Q1: Honestly, my study routine was pretty chaotic. I'd tell myself I'd sit down and work through materials systematically, but then anxiety would kick in and I'd end up avoiding things for days. When I did study, it was usually late at night when the house was quiet, reading through notes I'd printed out. I'd aim for maybe two or three hours at a time, but my focus would drift. With sixteen courses going on, I just kind of bounced between whatever felt least overwhelming in the moment.
Q2: My pattern varied wildly depending on how scared I was about upcoming deadlines. Some weeks I'd barely touch anything because the thought of how much I had to do paralyzed me. Then I'd have these frantic bursts where I'd try to cram everything in at once. Regular times? That's a nice idea, but between my extracurriculars and just... life, I couldn't maintain any real consistency. Deadlines were basically the only thing that forced structure onto my week.
Q3: Usually fear, to be completely honest. I'd log in when I suddenly remembered an assignment was due or when I got that sinking feeling that I'd missed something important. Sometimes I'd log in just to check if there were any announcements that might affect me. The gamified tasks they introduced made me curious sometimes, so I'd peek at those. But mostly it was anxiety-driven check-ins rather than any real enthusiasm to learn.
Q4: Not really, no. There were brief periods where I'd tell myself 'okay, I'm going to check the LMS every morning with my coffee,' but that lasted maybe a week before I fell off. The gamified elements they added were interesting at first and got me logging in more frequently for a bit. But it never became automatic for me. The LMS always felt like something I had to force myself to face rather than something that fit naturally into my day.
Q5: The reading materials and written content were most useful to me since that's how I learn best. I could download PDFs, print them out, and work through them at my own pace with a highlighter. The discussion forums were occasionally helpful when other students asked questions I was too scared to ask myself. I appreciated when instructors posted detailed written explanations rather than just videos because I could actually engage with text without feeling so overwhelmed.
Q6: I pretty much ignored most of the video content, which I know is bad since there was almost five hours of it I apparently watched but honestly a lot of that was just playing in the background. The peer interaction stuff I avoided because talking to other students makes me anxious. I'd delay discussion posts until the absolute last minute because putting my thoughts out there for others to see felt terrifying. The gamified tasks were hit or miss—sometimes the game elements felt patronizing.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time when I could clearly see how it connected to what I'd be assessed on. If I couldn't see the point or if it felt like busy work, I'd deprioritize it immediately. Anything that let me work independently at my own pace felt more worthwhile than collaborative stuff. I also valued activities that didn't require me to expose my uncertainty to others. If I could learn quietly on my own and then demonstrate knowledge later, that felt safer and more productive.
Q8: Optional activities felt like traps, honestly. Like, are they really optional or will I regret not doing them later? That uncertainty made me anxious, so I'd often ignore them entirely rather than deal with that ambiguity. Required activities at least gave me clear expectations—I knew I had to do them, so the decision was made for me. Optional stuff just added to the mental load of figuring out what actually mattered. I ended up doing very few optional things because I was already drowning.
Q9: Oh, absolutely. Most of my actual studying happened offline with printed materials, notebooks, and sometimes textbooks I'd borrowed. I'd download readings and then work through them away from my computer because screens make my anxiety worse when I'm trying to concentrate. I also used some external YouTube videos and websites when the course materials didn't click for me. The LMS only ever saw a fraction of the time I actually spent trying to understand things.
Q10: There were many nights where I'd sit with my printed notes for hours, re-reading and making handwritten summaries, and none of that showed up anywhere. I'd also think through problems while doing other things—walking to my extracurricular activities, lying in bed unable to sleep because of anxiety. Sometimes I'd discuss concepts with a friend over coffee, working through ideas verbally. The LMS just saw my sporadic logins, not the mental wrestling I did constantly.
Q11: Yes, this was basically my standard approach. I'd access a reading or resource once, download it immediately, and then never open it in the LMS again. I'd work from the downloaded file for days or weeks. This means my engagement looked minimal online when really I was returning to those materials repeatedly offline. It was just easier for me to work with physical copies or saved files than to keep logging back into the platform every time I wanted to review something.
Q12: There were definitely periods where everything felt impossible. When my anxiety spiked, I'd avoid the course entirely because even looking at the LMS made my heart race. Around midterms when everything piled up across all sixteen courses, I basically shut down for a while. My extracurricular commitments sometimes collided with academic demands too. When I felt scared and overwhelmed, my instinct was to hide from the problem rather than face it, which obviously made things worse.
Q13: Constantly. The workload across all my courses was genuinely unmanageable sometimes, and the LMS became something I'd dread opening. When I was exhausted or confused about what was even expected, I'd just... not engage. Competing responsibilities from my extracurriculars added pressure too. There were weeks where I'd log in, feel immediately overwhelmed by all the notifications and pending tasks, and log right back out without doing anything meaningful. Fatigue made everything feel insurmountable.
Q14: More often than I'd like to admit. I'd open videos and let them play while my mind wandered somewhere else entirely. I'd scroll through readings without actually processing the words because I was too anxious to concentrate. Sometimes I'd click through materials just to make the 'incomplete' markers go away, even though I wasn't really learning anything. It was like going through the motions to feel less guilty rather than actually engaging with the content.
Q15: All the time. Assignments especially—I'd know they were important and still put them off because starting them meant confronting how much I didn't understand. The fear of doing it wrong was sometimes worse than the fear of not doing it at all, which makes no logical sense but that's anxiety for you. I'd tell myself I'd start tomorrow, and then tomorrow would come and I'd find another reason to delay. My assignment completion rate shows exactly how much I struggled with this.
Q16: Deadlines were basically the only thing that got me to actually submit work. I'd have these last-minute panic sessions where I'd finally force myself to engage because there was no more time to avoid it. The LMS would probably show spikes in my activity right before due dates and then nothing for days after. It wasn't healthy, but that fear of missing a deadline was often stronger than my fear of engaging with difficult material. Without deadlines, I probably would have done even less.
Q17: Feedback was complicated for me. When I got positive feedback, it helped a little with my confidence. But negative or even constructive feedback could send me into a spiral where I'd avoid that course for days. I'd sometimes delay looking at my grades because I was scared of what I'd see. The gamified feedback was less threatening somehow—points and badges felt less personal than written comments on my work. But overall, feedback made me more anxious than motivated.
Q18: The course structure felt overwhelming with so much content spread across the platform. I often felt lost about what I should prioritize, which fed into my avoidance patterns. When modules were clearly laid out with explicit expectations, I did better. But when things felt ambiguous or when there were too many optional paths, I'd freeze up. The gamified elements added some structure that helped initially, but they couldn't overcome my fundamental struggle with the volume of material.
Q19: Having too many courses running simultaneously made everything harder—sixteen online courses is genuinely too much to track. The aspects that helped were clear, written instructions and downloadable materials I could work with offline. What made it harder was anything requiring real-time participation or peer interaction, which triggered my anxiety. The gamification was a mixed bag—sometimes motivating, sometimes it felt like the system was trying to trick me into engaging rather than giving me genuine reasons to care.
Q20: I can actually relate to this. Sometimes you're clicking around trying to understand what's expected, watching videos to figure out what's going on, reading discussions to see if others are as confused as you are—but none of that translates into submitted work. The gap between consuming content and producing something is huge when you're anxious. You might be very active in the system while being completely paralyzed when it comes to actually completing and submitting assignments.
Q21: That definitely reflects part of my experience. I passed despite my low LMS engagement because a lot of my actual learning happened offline. I'd study from printed materials, think through concepts on my own, and then just show up for exams having prepared in ways the system couldn't track. My exam score was decent even though my assignment completion was poor. The LMS engagement metrics don't capture studying that happens away from the computer, which for me was most of it.
Q22: Course logs can show when you logged in and what you clicked on, which gives a rough picture of your online behavior patterns. But they miss everything that happens offline—the reading, the thinking, the struggling with concepts at 2 AM. They also can't tell the difference between genuine engagement and anxious clicking around without real comprehension. They miss the emotional experience entirely. My logs probably show someone barely present, but they don't show someone who was scared and trying in ways that weren't visible.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about understanding the material, not just going through the motions to get a grade. It means thinking about concepts outside of designated study time, making connections to other things you know, wanting to learn more. For me, genuine engagement was rare this semester because anxiety kept getting in the way. I was more often in survival mode—doing the minimum to pass—than truly engaged with learning for its own sake. Real engagement requires mental space I didn't always have.
Q24: The LMS can't show how scared I was most of the time, or how much energy it took just to log in on bad days. It can't show the hours I spent with printed readings away from my computer, or the way I'd think about course concepts while doing other things. It doesn't capture the internal battle between wanting to engage and being too anxious to face the material. My low activity looks like not caring, but really it was often caring too much and being paralyzed by that. The fear doesn't show up in the data.
